enrichment.short_description: Schleuder is an open-source, self-hosted mailing list manager that encrypts group email with OpenPGP.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Schleuder is a self-hosted, GPG-enabled mailing list manager. It acts as an email gateway for a group: subscribers exchange OpenPGP-encrypted messages among themselves, the list can receive email from non-subscribers, and subscribers can send mail out to non-subscribers through the list address, with Schleuder handling encryption and decryption on each hop.

Each list has its own key pair, and subscriber public keys are stored and validated on the server. Administration is done primarily through commands sent by email, which keeps the tool usable without a dedicated web dashboard, though an optional web interface (schleuder-web) can be run against its API. Schleuder is written in Ruby and depends on GnuPG and SQLite.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Schleuder is aimed at privacy-conscious groups, activist and journalist collectives, and technical teams who already use GPG and want an encrypted alternative to standard mailing list managers for sensitive group communication.

features.title: OpenPGP encryption gateway
features.description: Decrypts incoming mail and re-encrypts a copy for each subscriber using their own public key, so no single party other than the list server handles plaintext for the whole group.

features.title: Send to and receive from non-subscribers
features.description: List members can email outsiders through the list address, and the list can accept incoming mail from non-subscribers, with encryption applied automatically where keys are known.

features.title: Email-command administration
features.description: Subscribers and administrators manage keys, membership, and list settings by sending structured commands via email, without requiring a separate admin panel.

features.title: Per-subscriber key management
features.description: Each subscriber's OpenPGP public key is stored on the list and used automatically when encrypting outgoing copies of a message.

features.title: Optional web interface via API
features.description: The companion schleuder-web project can provide a browser-based front end that talks to Schleuder's API for list and subscriber management.

features.title: Self-hosted Ruby application
features.description: Runs on your own server using Ruby, GnuPG 2.2+, and SQLite, giving operators full control over where encrypted mail is processed and stored.

pros: Provides real end-to-end style encryption for group mailing lists, not just transport encryption
Works entirely over standard email, so subscribers don't need a special client
Self-hosted, so encrypted group communication stays on infrastructure you control
Actively maintained by a privacy-focused open-source community
Integrates with subscribers' existing OpenPGP keys
cons: Requires a technical setup (Ruby, GnuPG, SQLite) and comfort with command-line administration
No polished web GUI out of the box; the web interface is a separate, optional project
Email-command based administration has a learning curve for non-technical subscribers
Smaller community and ecosystem than mainstream mailing list managers like Sympa or Mailman

faqs.question: What is Schleuder used for?
faqs.answer: It's used to run encrypted group mailing lists, so members can exchange OpenPGP-encrypted email through a shared list address instead of a standard, unencrypted mailing list.

faqs.question: Does Schleuder require GPG?
faqs.answer: Yes. Schleuder relies on GnuPG to encrypt and decrypt messages for each subscriber's own key.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Schleuder?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is self-hosted software written in Ruby, requiring Ruby, GnuPG, and SQLite on a server you control.

faqs.question: Does Schleuder work with existing PGP keys?
faqs.answer: Yes, subscribers add their existing OpenPGP public key to the list, which Schleuder then uses to encrypt their copy of each message.

use_cases.title: Encrypted coordination for activist and journalist groups
use_cases.description: Groups handling sensitive information can run a mailing list where every message is encrypted per subscriber, reducing exposure if the mail server is compromised.

use_cases.title: Sensitive security or incident-response lists
use_cases.description: Technical teams that already use GPG for other purposes can use Schleuder for internal security discussions that need encryption at rest and in transit.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Schleuder is a free, self-hosted mailing list manager that encrypts group email with OpenPGP so subscribers can communicate privately.

enrichment.short_description: Sympa is an open-source mailing list manager built for organizations running large-scale email lists.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sympa (SYsteme de Multi-Postage Automatique) is an open-source mailing list management system first released in 1997 and now maintained by the Sympa Community. It automates subscription handling, message moderation, and archiving, and is built to perform well on large lists — the project cites being able to send a message to 90% of subscribers of a 20,000-member list within about five minutes.

Administration and list browsing happen through a web interface, with a SOAP API available for programmatic access and command-line tools for server administrators. Sympa supports DKIM and ARC email authentication, customizable subscriber data sources, and translations into a number of languages. It is written primarily in Perl.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Sympa is aimed at universities, research networks, and organizations that need to run and moderate many mailing lists at scale, historically with strong adoption among French and European academic and research institutions.

features.title: Web-based list management
features.description: Administrators and moderators manage subscriptions, list settings, and message moderation through a browser interface.

features.title: Archive browsing
features.description: Mailing list archives are browsable through the web interface, giving members a searchable history of list activity.

features.title: DKIM and ARC support
features.description: Built-in support for DKIM signing and ARC helps outgoing list mail pass modern email authentication checks.

features.title: SOAP API
features.description: Provides a SOAP API for integrating list management and subscription actions with external systems.

features.title: Scalable message distribution
features.description: Designed to distribute messages efficiently to large subscriber bases without excessive delay.

features.title: Command-line administration
features.description: Server administrators can perform maintenance and configuration tasks through command-line tools alongside the web UI.

pros: Handles very large mailing lists efficiently
Long track record, in active development since 1997
Web interface for both administrators and subscribers
Free and open source with an active community
SOAP API for integration with other systems
cons: Setup and configuration are more involved than lightweight mailing list tools
Interface feels dated compared to newer SaaS email tools
Best documentation and community support skew toward academic/research use cases
Requires a Perl runtime and its own set of system dependencies to self-host

faqs.question: Is Sympa free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sympa is free, open-source software released under the GPL, with no licensing cost.

faqs.question: Can Sympa handle large mailing lists?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is specifically designed for performance on large lists, with the project citing delivery to 90% of a 20,000-member list in about five minutes.

faqs.question: Does Sympa have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it exposes a SOAP API for programmatic list and subscription management.

faqs.question: Who maintains Sympa?
faqs.answer: Sympa is maintained by The Sympa Community; earlier development was carried out by RENATER and, before that, the Comité Réseau des Universités (CRU).

use_cases.title: University and research mailing lists
use_cases.description: Institutions can run large numbers of department, course, or research group mailing lists with self-service subscription and moderation.

use_cases.title: Large member or association newsletters
use_cases.description: Organizations with tens of thousands of members can distribute list mail reliably while keeping full control of subscriber data on their own servers.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sympa is a free, open-source mailing list manager built for organizations that need to run and moderate large-scale email lists.

enrichment.short_description: Cypht is a free, self-hosted webmail app that combines multiple email accounts and RSS feeds into one unified inbox.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cypht is an open-source webmail application that aggregates messages from multiple email accounts into a single unified view, functioning somewhat like a news reader but for email. It supports IMAP/SMTP, JMAP, and EWS (Exchange Web Services), so it can pull mail from a mix of account types into one interface.

The application is built around a modular system of 'module sets' that add or extend functionality, and it supports IMAP, LDAP, and OAuth authentication. Cypht also includes Sieve-based server-side filtering, universal search across connected accounts and RSS feeds, and cross-account message actions. It is written in PHP and JavaScript, licensed under LGPL-2.1, and free to self-host.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Cypht suits people and organizations who manage several email accounts (personal, work, multiple domains) and want one lightweight, self-hosted interface instead of switching between separate webmail logins, plus admins who want a low-overhead alternative to heavier webmail clients.

features.title: Unified multi-account inbox
features.description: Combines messages from several connected email accounts into a single consolidated view.

features.title: Multi-protocol support
features.description: Connects to accounts over IMAP/SMTP, JMAP, and EWS, covering both traditional and modern mail backends.

features.title: Universal search
features.description: Searches across all connected email accounts and RSS feeds from one search box.

features.title: Sieve-based filtering
features.description: Supports server-side Sieve filters so mail can be organized even when the client is offline.

features.title: Flexible authentication
features.description: Supports IMAP, LDAP, and OAuth-based authentication for connecting accounts.

features.title: Modular architecture
features.description: Functionality is delivered through module sets, letting administrators enable only the features they need.

pros: Lightweight compared to full-featured webmail suites
Handles multiple accounts and protocols (IMAP, JMAP, EWS) in one interface
Combines email with RSS feed reading
Free, open source, and self-hosted
Modular design keeps the footprint small
cons: Interface is more utilitarian than polished, consumer-style webmail clients
Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem than Roundcube
JMAP/EWS support depends on the mail server's own protocol implementation
Best suited to users comfortable with self-hosting PHP applications

faqs.question: Is Cypht free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cypht is free, open-source software licensed under LGPL-2.1.

faqs.question: Can Cypht combine multiple email accounts?
faqs.answer: Yes, its core feature is aggregating multiple IMAP/SMTP, JMAP, and EWS accounts into one unified inbox.

faqs.question: Does Cypht support RSS feeds?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can pull in RSS feeds alongside email so both appear in the same unified interface and search.

faqs.question: What is Cypht built with?
faqs.answer: It's built primarily in PHP with JavaScript for the front end, designed to be lightweight for self-hosted deployment.

use_cases.title: Consolidating multiple email accounts
use_cases.description: Users with several personal or work email addresses across different providers can read and search all of them from one self-hosted inbox.
use_cases.audience: Individuals and small teams managing multiple mailboxes

use_cases.title: Lightweight self-hosted webmail for organizations
use_cases.description: Admins who want a low-resource webmail option for staff, without the overhead of a heavier full-suite client, can deploy Cypht on modest hosting.
use_cases.audience: IT admins running self-hosted mail infrastructure

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cypht is a free, open-source webmail app that unifies multiple IMAP, JMAP, EWS email accounts and RSS feeds into one self-hosted inbox.

enrichment.short_description: Roundcube is a free, open-source, browser-based IMAP email client with a desktop-application-style interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Roundcube is a browser-based, multilingual IMAP email client designed to feel like a desktop application. It offers drag-and-drop message handling, full MIME and HTML message support, an address book with LDAP connectors, spell checking, PGP encryption support, and a responsive layout for different screen sizes.

The software is free and open source, licensed under GPLv3 with a plugin exception that lets developers build plugins and skins without triggering GPL obligations on their own code. Roundcube is written in PHP and JavaScript, stores its data in MariaDB, MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite, and is commonly deployed via source packages or Docker containers alongside a mail server such as Dovecot or Postfix.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Roundcube is one of the most widely deployed self-hosted webmail clients, used by hosting providers, businesses, and individuals who run their own mail servers and want a modern, plugin-extensible browser interface for their users.

features.title: Desktop-style IMAP client in the browser
features.description: Provides an application-like interface for browsing, composing, and managing IMAP mailboxes from any modern browser.

features.title: Address book with LDAP support
features.description: Built-in contact management that can connect to LDAP directories in addition to local contacts.

features.title: Plugin and skin architecture
features.description: A documented plugin API and skin system let administrators extend functionality and customize the interface without modifying core code.

features.title: PGP encryption support
features.description: Supports PGP-based message encryption and signing through its plugin ecosystem for users who need end-to-end encrypted email.

features.title: Drag-and-drop message management
features.description: Messages can be organized between folders using drag-and-drop, similar to a desktop mail client.

features.title: Multi-database backend support
features.description: Works with MariaDB, MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite for storing user preferences, contacts, and cached data.

pros: Mature, widely deployed project with a large plugin ecosystem
Desktop-like usability inside a browser
Free, open source, self-hostable alongside any IMAP mail server
Actively maintained with regular security updates
Multilingual interface
cons: Requires a separate IMAP/SMTP mail server; Roundcube itself is only the webmail front end
Default skin looks dated unless a modern theme/plugin is applied
Plugin quality varies since many are third-party contributions
Self-hosting requires ongoing maintenance (PHP, database, security patches)

faqs.question: Is Roundcube free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Roundcube is free, open-source software licensed under GPLv3 with a plugin exception.

faqs.question: Does Roundcube include its own mail server?
faqs.answer: No, Roundcube is a webmail client only — it connects to an existing IMAP/SMTP mail server such as Dovecot and Postfix.

faqs.question: Can Roundcube be extended with plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, it has a documented plugin API and skin system used by a large ecosystem of community plugins.

faqs.question: How is Roundcube typically deployed?
faqs.answer: It's commonly deployed from source packages or as a Docker container on a server that already runs an IMAP mail service.

use_cases.title: Webmail for self-hosted mail servers
use_cases.description: Organizations running their own IMAP/SMTP infrastructure can offer users a browser-based inbox without relying on third-party webmail.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious email access
use_cases.description: Users who want browser-based access to a self-hosted mailbox with optional PGP support can use Roundcube instead of a cloud webmail provider.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Roundcube is a free, open-source, browser-based IMAP webmail client with a desktop-style interface and a large plugin ecosystem.

enrichment.short_description: SnappyMail is a free, self-hosted, lightweight webmail client forked from RainLoop for modern browsers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SnappyMail is a lightweight, self-hosted webmail client maintained by djmaze as a fork and continuation of the discontinued RainLoop project. It focuses on speed and a small footprint, with the client reported to download around 138 KB with Brotli compression and to score highly on Lighthouse performance audits.

It supports IMAP and SMTP (POP3 support was removed from the RainLoop codebase), a Sieve script editor for server-side filtering, PGP encryption, and dark mode, while deliberately avoiding tracking features like Gravatar or social-media integrations. It runs on PHP 7.4+ with the mbstring extension and needs no database to install, and is licensed under AGPLv3.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: SnappyMail suits self-hosters and small mail providers who want a fast, minimal webmail client with modern browser support rather than a heavier, more feature-dense option like Roundcube.

features.title: Lightweight, fast client
features.description: Small compressed download size and strong Lighthouse performance scores keep the interface responsive, especially on modest hardware.

features.title: IMAP and SMTP support
features.description: Connects to standard IMAP mailboxes and sends mail over SMTP; POP3 was removed after the RainLoop fork.

features.title: Sieve filter editor
features.description: Includes an advanced editor for managing server-side Sieve filtering rules directly from the webmail interface.

features.title: PGP encryption
features.description: Supports PGP-based message encryption and signing for users who need encrypted email.

features.title: No database required
features.description: Can be installed and run without setting up a separate database server, simplifying deployment.

features.title: Privacy-focused design
features.description: Avoids integrations such as Gravatar and social-media widgets to limit third-party data exposure.

pros: Very lightweight and fast compared to heavier webmail clients
No database dependency, simplifying self-hosted setup
Actively maintained continuation of the popular RainLoop project
Free and open source under AGPLv3
Privacy-conscious defaults with no tracking integrations
cons: No POP3 support, which was dropped from the original RainLoop codebase
Smaller plugin/theme ecosystem than Roundcube
Requires a modern browser (2020+); older browsers, including legacy Edge and IE, aren't supported
Still requires a separate IMAP/SMTP mail server to connect to

faqs.question: Is SnappyMail free?
faqs.answer: Yes, SnappyMail is free, open-source software licensed under AGPLv3.

faqs.question: Is SnappyMail related to RainLoop?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's a fork and continuation of RainLoop Webmail, rebuilt and modernized for current browsers after RainLoop's development stalled.

faqs.question: Does SnappyMail need a database?
faqs.answer: No, it can be installed and run without a separate database server.

faqs.question: What PHP version does SnappyMail require?
faqs.answer: PHP 7.4 or higher, with the mbstring extension enabled.

use_cases.title: Fast webmail for self-hosted mail servers
use_cases.description: Admins who want a responsive, low-overhead webmail front end for an existing IMAP/SMTP mail server can deploy SnappyMail instead of heavier alternatives.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused email access
use_cases.description: Users who want a webmail client without tracking integrations, plus optional PGP encryption, can use SnappyMail for day-to-day email access.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SnappyMail is a free, open-source, lightweight self-hosted webmail client forked from RainLoop for modern browsers.

enrichment.short_description: SquirrelMail is a long-running, free, open-source webmail client, now maintained only through occasional SVN snapshots.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SquirrelMail is a browser-based email client, branded 'Webmail for Nuts!', that has been used to add a web front end to IMAP mailboxes since the early 2000s. It supports a plugin system covering authentication methods, attachment handling, message composition, and security features such as S/MIME verification.

The project has not had a numbered release since version 1.4.22 in 2011. Since then it has continued only as SVN snapshots aimed at keeping the codebase compatible with newer PHP versions, and some hosting providers have publicly announced they no longer support it. Prospective users should weigh this against more actively developed alternatives such as Roundcube or SnappyMail.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: SquirrelMail is now relevant mainly to legacy deployments that already run it and to hobbyists interested in a very lightweight, dependency-light webmail client — new self-hosted mail projects should generally consider a more actively maintained client.

features.title: Plugin-based architecture
features.description: Functionality such as authentication options, attachment handling, and message composition tools is added through a plugin system.

features.title: S/MIME support via plugins
features.description: Security-related plugins add capabilities like S/MIME message verification.

features.title: Low resource footprint
features.description: Designed to run with minimal server resources, which historically made it popular on shared hosting.

features.title: Multiple folder support
features.description: Supports IMAP folder browsing and per-user preference configuration.

pros: Extremely lightweight with minimal server resource requirements
Long history and broad familiarity from its widespread past adoption
Free and open source
Plugin system allows some functional customization
cons: No official numbered release since 2011; current code ships only as SVN development snapshots
Development activity is minimal compared to actively maintained clients like Roundcube
Several hosting providers have dropped support and recommend migrating away from it
Dated user interface compared to modern webmail clients

faqs.question: Is SquirrelMail still maintained?
faqs.answer: Only minimally — there has been no numbered release since 1.4.22 in 2011, though the project continues to publish SVN snapshots for PHP compatibility and occasional fixes.

faqs.question: Is SquirrelMail free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free, open-source software; the project also accepts donations.

faqs.question: Should I choose SquirrelMail for a new deployment?
faqs.answer: Given its limited ongoing maintenance, most new self-hosted webmail deployments are better served by an actively developed alternative such as Roundcube or SnappyMail.

faqs.question: Does SquirrelMail support plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, a range of community plugins extend authentication, attachments, composition, and security features.

use_cases.title: Maintaining legacy webmail deployments
use_cases.description: Organizations already running SquirrelMail can keep it operating on updated PHP versions using the current SVN snapshots.

use_cases.title: Minimal-resource webmail experiments
use_cases.description: Hobbyists on very constrained hosting can use SquirrelMail's lightweight footprint for a basic webmail front end.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: SquirrelMail is a free, open-source webmail client with a long history, now maintained only through occasional SVN snapshots.

enrichment.short_description: Ergo is a free, open-source IRC server written in Go that bundles services and bouncer-style history into one binary.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ergo is a modern IRC server (IRCd) written in Go, distributed under the MIT license. It combines traditional server functionality with integrated services — NickServ for account management, ChanServ for channel registration, and HostServ for vanity hosts — plus bouncer-style features that store and replay message history and let multiple clients share a nickname.

The project emphasizes simple deployment as a single binary, broad IRCv3 protocol support intended to make it usable as a reference implementation, and runtime-reloadable YAML configuration. It supports SASL authentication, optional LDAP integration, TLS with client certificates, IP cloaking, and Tor hidden service compatibility.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Ergo is aimed at communities and administrators who want to run a modern, easy-to-deploy IRC network with built-in account services and message history, without stitching together a separate ircd, services daemon, and bouncer.

features.title: Integrated services
features.description: NickServ, ChanServ, and HostServ are built into the server, so account registration and channel management don't require a separate services package.

features.title: Bouncer-style message history
features.description: Stores and replays chat history, letting multiple clients or reconnects pick up recent conversation like a bouncer would.

features.title: Modern IRCv3 support
features.description: Implements a broad set of IRCv3 specifications, positioning Ergo as a reference implementation for modern IRC clients.

features.title: Single-binary deployment
features.description: Ships as a self-contained Go binary, simplifying installation compared to multi-component IRC stacks.

features.title: Runtime-reloadable YAML config
features.description: Server configuration is defined in YAML and can be reloaded without restarting the server.

features.title: SASL and optional LDAP auth
features.description: Supports SASL authentication and can integrate with LDAP for account verification.

pros: Single binary bundles ircd, services, and bouncer-like history in one deployment
Modern IRCv3 support suitable as a reference implementation
Free, open source under the permissive MIT license
Runtime-reloadable configuration reduces downtime for config changes
Built-in TLS, SASL, and Tor hidden service support
cons: IRC as a protocol has a smaller mainstream user base than modern chat platforms
No official managed hosting; operators run and maintain their own server
Bundling services into the ircd is a different architecture than the traditional ircd + services split, which may not fit networks wanting that separation
Documentation and community are smaller than long-established IRC servers like InspIRCd

faqs.question: Is Ergo free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Ergo is free, open-source software licensed under the MIT license.

faqs.question: What language is Ergo written in?
faqs.answer: Go, which lets it ship as a single self-contained binary.

faqs.question: Does Ergo include IRC services like NickServ?
faqs.answer: Yes, NickServ, ChanServ, and HostServ are built directly into the server rather than requiring a separate services package.

faqs.question: Can Ergo replay chat history like a bouncer?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes bouncer-style history storage and replay, and supports multiple clients sharing a nickname.

use_cases.title: Running a small-to-mid IRC community
use_cases.description: Communities can stand up a modern IRC network with accounts, channel registration, and history in one deployment instead of assembling separate ircd and services software.
use_cases.audience: IRC network operators and community admins

use_cases.title: Self-hosted team chat over IRC
use_cases.description: Technical teams that prefer IRC over modern chat apps can run Ergo for lightweight, persistent group chat with bouncer-style history.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ergo is a free, open-source IRC server written in Go that bundles account services and bouncer-style chat history into one binary.

enrichment.short_description: InspIRCd is a free, open-source, modular IRC server (IRCv3-compliant) for UNIX-like systems and Windows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: InspIRCd is a high-performance IRC server (IRCd) for UNIX-like and Windows systems, licensed under GPLv2. It's built around a modular architecture, so administrators enable only the modules they need, and it supports the ratified IRCv3 specifications for compatibility with modern IRC clients.

The project provides installation paths for Debian, Ubuntu, RHEL, Docker, Windows, and source builds, and is maintained by the InspIRCd Contributors with ongoing releases — version 4.11.0 shipped in June 2026. InspIRCd powers more than 1,000 IRC networks, including HybridIRC, Snoonet, and Teranova.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: InspIRCd is aimed at network operators who want a stable, modular, actively maintained IRC server that runs on both Linux/Unix and Windows and can scale to large, established networks.

features.title: Modular architecture
features.description: Server features are provided as modules, so operators can enable exactly the functionality their network needs and keep the core lightweight.

features.title: IRCv3 compliance
features.description: Supports all ratified IRCv3 specifications, keeping the server compatible with modern IRC clients and bouncers.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on UNIX-like systems as well as Windows, with documented install paths for Debian, Ubuntu, RHEL, Docker, and source builds.

features.title: Proven at scale
features.description: Powers over 1,000 IRC networks, including established communities like Snoonet and HybridIRC.

features.title: Active release cadence
features.description: Maintained by the InspIRCd Contributors with regular version releases and up-to-date documentation.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Official Docker support simplifies containerized deployment for self-hosters.

pros: Modular design keeps footprint lean while allowing extensive customization
Runs on both UNIX-like systems and Windows
Proven in production on networks with large user bases
Actively maintained with regular releases
Free and open source under GPLv2
cons: Configuration and module system have a learning curve for first-time IRC operators
IRC networks still require separate services software (like Anope or Atheme) for account/channel registration
Documentation is comprehensive but geared toward operators already familiar with IRC concepts
No official managed hosting; operators self-host and maintain their own servers

faqs.question: Is InspIRCd free?
faqs.answer: Yes, InspIRCd is free, open-source software licensed under GPLv2.

faqs.question: Does InspIRCd run on Windows?
faqs.answer: Yes, it officially supports both UNIX-like systems and Windows.

faqs.question: How many networks use InspIRCd?
faqs.answer: The project reports more than 1,000 IRC networks running InspIRCd, including HybridIRC, Snoonet, and Teranova.

faqs.question: Is InspIRCd IRCv3 compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports all ratified IRCv3 specifications.

use_cases.title: Running an established IRC network
use_cases.description: Network operators managing large or growing communities can rely on InspIRCd's modular design and track record for stability at scale.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform self-hosted chat infrastructure
use_cases.description: Teams needing an IRC server that runs on both Linux and Windows infrastructure can standardize on InspIRCd across environments.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: InspIRCd is a free, open-source, modular IRCv3-compliant IRC server for UNIX-like systems and Windows, used by 1,000+ networks.

enrichment.short_description: Kiwi IRC is a free, open-source, browser-based IRC client that connects to networks without any download or plugin.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kiwi IRC is an open-source, web-based IRC client, licensed under Apache-2.0 and built with Vue.js. It's made up entirely of static files, so it can be deployed on any web server or CDN without a backend application server for the client itself. It connects through websocket-enabled IRC servers, the webircgateway proxy, or KiwiBNC for persistent, bouncer-like connections.

The client offers multiple layout options, light and dark themes, desktop notifications, text styling, and a plugin system that supports extensions like file uploading and video conferencing. It's used as the embedded web client by a number of established IRC networks, including Freenode (historically), EFnet, DALnet, Snoonet, and Rizon.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Kiwi IRC is built for IRC network operators who want to offer visitors an in-browser chat client without requiring a desktop app, and for anyone who wants to join an IRC channel instantly from a web page.

features.title: Static-file deployment
features.description: The client is entirely static files, so it can be hosted on any web server or CDN without a dedicated backend for the front end.

features.title: Multiple connection modes
features.description: Connects via websocket IRC servers, the webircgateway proxy, or KiwiBNC for persistent bouncer-style sessions.

features.title: Theming and layout options
features.description: Includes multiple layout choices along with light and dark themes for the interface.

features.title: Desktop notifications
features.description: Supports browser desktop notifications for new messages and mentions.

features.title: Plugin system
features.description: Extensible with plugins, including community additions for file uploads and video conferencing.

features.title: Single JSON runtime config
features.description: Client behavior and network settings are configured through one JSON file, applied at runtime.

pros: No installation required for end users — runs entirely in the browser
Free, open source under a permissive Apache-2.0 license
Simple, static-file deployment on any web host or CDN
Used and battle-tested by several large, established IRC networks
Extensible through a plugin system
cons: Requires a compatible connection method (websocket IRC server, webircgateway, or KiwiBNC) on the server side
No official managed/paid hosting; operators host and configure it themselves
As a client only, it depends on a separate IRC server or bouncer for the actual network
Advanced customization requires editing the JSON runtime config

faqs.question: Is Kiwi IRC free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Kiwi IRC is free, open-source software licensed under Apache-2.0.

faqs.question: Do I need to install anything to use Kiwi IRC?
faqs.answer: No, it runs entirely in the browser as a web-based client with no download or plugin required by end users.

faqs.question: What IRC networks use Kiwi IRC?
faqs.answer: It has been used as the embedded web client for several established networks, including EFnet, DALnet, Snoonet, and Rizon, among others.

faqs.question: Does Kiwi IRC need its own server?
faqs.answer: It needs a compatible backend connection — a websocket-capable IRC server, the webircgateway proxy, or KiwiBNC — since Kiwi IRC itself is only the client.

use_cases.title: In-browser chat widget for an IRC network
use_cases.description: Network operators can embed Kiwi IRC on their website so visitors can join channels instantly without installing a separate IRC client.
use_cases.audience: IRC network operators and community sites

use_cases.title: Lightweight team chat over IRC
use_cases.description: Teams that already run an IRC server can give members a friendly, no-install web client instead of requiring a desktop IRC app.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kiwi IRC is a free, open-source, browser-based IRC client used by several established networks, deployable as static files.

enrichment.short_description: ngIRCd is a free, open-source, lightweight IRC server for small or private networks, maintained since 2001.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ngIRCd is a lightweight, open-source IRC server aimed at small or private networks, able to run standalone or as part of a server cluster. It's licensed under the GPL and has been maintained by Alexander Barton and contributors since 2001, giving it a long track record of steady, incremental development.

The server supports IPv6 and SSL/TLS connections, PAM-based authentication, standard user and channel modes, host cloaking for privacy, and works with dynamic IP addresses, which suits home or small-office deployments. Written in portable C, it runs on Linux, macOS, BSD variants, and Windows, and the project states a basic setup can be ready for use in about five minutes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: ngIRCd is aimed at hobbyists, small communities, and admins who want a simple, low-maintenance IRC server for a private or small network rather than a large public IRC network with heavy services requirements.

features.title: IPv6 and SSL/TLS support
features.description: Supports modern IPv6 connectivity alongside encrypted SSL/TLS client connections.

features.title: PAM authentication
features.description: Can authenticate users against the system's PAM stack in addition to standard IRC password auth.

features.title: Host cloaking
features.description: Provides cloaking functionality to hide users' real hostnames or IP addresses for privacy.

features.title: Works with dynamic IPs
features.description: Designed to keep operating cleanly on connections with dynamic (changing) IP addresses, common on home networks.

features.title: Cross-platform C codebase
features.description: Portable C source runs on Linux, macOS, BSD variants, and Windows.

features.title: Server clustering
features.description: Can run as a standalone server or be linked with other ngIRCd instances into a network cluster.

pros: Simple to set up, with the project citing a working server in about five minutes
Low resource requirements, suited to small or home servers
Long-running, stable project maintained continuously since 2001
Cross-platform, including native Windows support
Free and open source under the GPL
cons: Aimed at small/private networks rather than large public IRC networks with heavy services needs
No built-in NickServ/ChanServ-style services; those require separate software
Smaller community and ecosystem than more widely used ircd projects
Documentation is functional but less extensive than larger IRC server projects

faqs.question: Is ngIRCd free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ngIRCd is free, open-source software licensed under the GPL.

faqs.question: How long has ngIRCd been developed?
faqs.answer: It has been maintained by Alexander Barton and contributors since 2001.

faqs.question: Does ngIRCd run on Windows?
faqs.answer: Yes, along with Linux, macOS, and BSD variants, thanks to its portable C codebase.

faqs.question: Is ngIRCd suited to large public IRC networks?
faqs.answer: It's primarily designed for small or private networks; very large public networks typically use servers built for that scale, like InspIRCd or Ergo.

use_cases.title: Private IRC server for a small community
use_cases.description: Hobbyists or small groups can run a lightweight, low-maintenance IRC server for private chat without the overhead of a large network stack.

use_cases.title: Home lab or self-hosted network chat
use_cases.description: Self-hosters running services on dynamic home IP addresses can use ngIRCd's dynamic-IP handling for a personal or family IRC server.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ngIRCd is a free, open-source, lightweight IRC server for small or private networks, maintained since 2001 and available for Linux, macOS, BSD, and Windows.
